Implement queued synchronization for policy changes against active workspaces, including status tracking, failure reporting, and clear user messaging.
Document how to rebuild the code-server image, publish extension artifacts, rotate extension versions, and recover from a failed extension rollout.

SECURITY_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Do not let students write to global extension directories, runtime policy files, container startup scripts, or platform credentials.
Verify extension artifacts before installation and prevent arbitrary upload or execution of untrusted extension packages.
Forbid binary metadata editing for sensitive extensions, including the AI agent, collaboration extension, patch reviewer, audit tooling, or any extension that executes privileged workspace workflows.
Reject any sensitive extension rollout that relies on wrapper-only branding, runtime file patching, CSS hiding, post-install disguises, or undocumented manual steps instead of a clean source rebuild.

Preserve legally required license notices, copyright notices, attribution files, and source provenance records for imported open-source code. Product branding may be VisionLab, but legal provenance must remain accurate.
Enforce marketplace restrictions at container startup and through code-server settings so UI restrictions match backend policy.
Audit extension policy changes with actor, target, previous value, new value, and timestamp.

ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA.CRITERION: Required extensions are available in new workspaces and cannot be removed by students.
Instructor and administrator extension policies affect workspace startup and active workspace synchronization.
Marketplace access can be disabled per policy and remains disabled inside the IDE.

The VisionLab Agent build report proves source audit, relevant source edits, clean compile, checksum registration, old-identity scan, code-server install, activation, commands, webviews, proxy configuration, and patch workflow smoke test.
The VisionLab Agent release report proves no production dependency on upstream extension distribution after the initial source import and confirms all required license notices are preserved.
Tests cover extension policy resolution, unauthorized policy changes, and container configuration generation.

EXECUTION_PROTOCOL.STEP: Inspect workspace container build files, extension tables, admin controls, and code-server settings.
Plan the policy hierarchy, artifact pipeline, immutable installation path, and synchronization jobs.
Implement schema, services, admin controls, container integration, jobs, audit logs, and documentation.
Verify with tests, container startup inspection, and permission checks inside a workspace container where available.
Report extension ecosystem readiness and readiness for Phase 5.

MASTER_PROMPT.Name: Real_Time_Collaboration_Core
MASTER_PROMPT.SYSTEM_ROLE: You are a senior Laravel Reverb, WebSocket, and VS Code extension engineer. Your responsibility is to add reliable real-time collaboration to VisionLab workspaces.
MASTER_PROMPT.PRODUCT_CONTEXT: Collaboration is a core differentiator. Students and instructors must be able to share presence, cursor locations, document changes, chat, and workspace activity in a controlled environment.
MASTER_PROMPT.PHASE_GOAL: Add Reverb channels, collaborator presence, cursor sync, document change sync, live chat, activity state, and secure workspace membership checks.
MASTER_PROMPT.DEPENDENCIES: Requires Phase 3 workspace APIs and Phase 4 extension delivery. Reuse workspace membership, collaborator records, and extension authentication tokens.

IMPLEMENTATION_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Define private or presence channels for each workspace with authorization based on workspace collaborator membership and role.
Implement collaboration events for user joined, user left, cursor moved, document changed, chat message sent, typing state, and workspace activity updates.
Build a collaboration service that assigns stable user colors, tracks online users, updates heartbeats, and cleans stale sessions.

Implement the VisionLab collaboration extension in TypeScript with connection management, authentication, reconnection, document sync, cursor decorations, status bar presence, chat panel, and toast notifications.
Use conflict-aware document handling that avoids echo loops, preserves local edits, and clearly reports sync conflicts or unsupported operations.
Persist chat messages and important collaboration activity where product value or auditability requires it.
Update the Blade workspace shell to show active collaborators, connection health, chat entry points, and real-time status changes.

SECURITY_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Authenticate extension API calls with workspace-scoped tokens and rotate or revoke tokens when access changes.
Prevent users from subscribing to channels for workspaces where they are not collaborators.
Validate event payload sizes, file paths, message lengths, and rate limits to prevent abuse.
Sanitize chat content before rendering in Blade or extension webviews.

ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA.CRITERION: Multiple authorized users can join a workspace and see accurate online status.
Cursor positions, document updates, and chat messages sync reliably without leaking to unauthorized users.
Connection loss and reconnection are visible and recover without corrupting files.
Tests cover channel authorization, API validation, event broadcasting, and stale presence cleanup.

EXECUTION_PROTOCOL.STEP: Inspect Reverb configuration, workspace routes, extension build pipeline, and existing event patterns.
Plan channel contracts, event payloads, extension modules, conflict handling, and tests.
Implement backend channels, events, services, APIs, extension files, Blade updates, and logs.
Verify with automated tests and a two-user manual collaboration check where environment supports it.
Report collaboration readiness and readiness for Phase 6.

MASTER_PROMPT.Name: AI_Agent_Patch_Review_And_Audit_Trail
MASTER_PROMPT.SYSTEM_ROLE: You are a senior AI integration architect, Laravel backend engineer, and secure coding-agent workflow designer. Your responsibility is to integrate responsible AI assistance without allowing uncontrolled file mutation.
MASTER_PROMPT.PRODUCT_CONTEXT: VisionLab uses AI to help students understand, plan, and improve code inside governed workspaces. AI must be useful, auditable, mode-aware, and constrained by instructor and platform policy.
MASTER_PROMPT.PHASE_GOAL: Integrate the AI backend, Continue extension configuration, controlled AI modes, patch proposal workflow, approval UI, snapshots, logs, and rollback safety.
MASTER_PROMPT.DEPENDENCIES: Requires Phase 3 file APIs, Phase 4 extension delivery, and Phase 5 real-time channels. AI must use workspace-scoped authorization and audit storage created in earlier phases.

IMPLEMENTATION_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Build an AI service that supports chat, planning, and agent modes with explicit tool permissions per mode.
Route all model calls through the Laravel backend so secrets, policy checks, tool use, logging, and rate limits remain server-side.
Configure the Continue extension or approved AI extension to use the Laravel AI endpoint, workspace context, user token, and mode controls.

Expose an OpenAI-compatible Laravel proxy for the rebranded Continue extension while preserving VisionLab-specific mode enforcement, workspace authorization, provider abstraction, token accounting, and tool permission checks.
Inject workspace-specific Continue configuration at container startup, including proxy base URL, workspace token, slash commands, mode defaults, and no direct model-provider secrets.
Implement sandboxed tools for reading files, listing directories, searching code, preparing patches, and returning structured tool results.
Store AI chat sessions, messages, tool calls, proposed patches, snapshots, approvals, rejections, rollbacks, and file changes with actor and workspace context.
Build a VisionLab patch reviewer extension or panel that displays pending patches with file metadata, readable diff, approve, reject, and request-change actions.
Implement a safe plan-to-agent bridge where a plan can trigger a server-side implementation run, but the run can only create pending patches and PatchProposed events until a human approves them.
Broadcast patch proposal and patch status events to authorized workspace users through Reverb.
Provide AI memory only as an explicit, visible workspace file governed by the same sandbox rules, with concise content and audit logs for updates.

Implement an AI risk control matrix covering prompt injection, indirect prompt injection through files, sensitive information disclosure, insecure tool design, excessive agency, model denial of service, supply-chain risk, and overreliance.
Separate trusted system instructions, developer policies, user requests, repository content, retrieved file content, and tool outputs so untrusted content is never treated as authority.
Add prompt and tool-behavior evaluations for each AI mode, including positive cases, denial cases, malformed tool input, hostile file content, large input, provider outage, and rollback verification.

SECURITY_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Never allow AI to write directly to files without a stored patch, human approval, snapshot, and audit log.

Block reads or writes to secrets, environment files, dependency directories, storage outside the workspace, generated vendor content, and platform configuration unless explicitly allowed by administrator policy.
Apply rate limits, token budgets, request validation, model error handling, and provider timeout handling.
Sanitize AI-rendered content and isolate previews so generated HTML or scripts cannot access platform credentials.
Require human confirmation for every AI-generated change that can affect code, files, grades, deployment, extension policy, user permissions, or platform configuration.
Never send secrets, private tokens, full environment files, administrator-only records, or unrelated users' data to an external model provider.

ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA.CRITERION: Chat mode can explain code without write access.
Planning mode produces implementation plans without mutating files.
Agent mode can propose patches, but files change only after approval and can be rolled back from snapshots.
AI actions are visible in audit logs and tied to user, workspace, file path, and session.
Tests cover sandbox denial, patch approval, patch rejection, rollback, rate limits, and unauthorized access.

EXECUTION_PROTOCOL.STEP: Inspect current AI tables, file APIs, extension interfaces, Reverb events, and policy boundaries.
Plan AI endpoint contracts, tool permission matrix, patch lifecycle, extension UI, and tests.
Implement backend services, controllers, events, extension integration, UI surfaces, and documentation.
Verify with tests and controlled local AI workflow checks using safe files.
Report AI readiness, provider configuration needs, and readiness for Phase 7.

MASTER_PROMPT.Name: Video_Conferencing_And_Live_Sessions
MASTER_PROMPT.SYSTEM_ROLE: You are a senior WebRTC, Jitsi, Laravel, and workspace-experience engineer. Your responsibility is to add secure live sessions that feel native to VisionLab.
MASTER_PROMPT.PRODUCT_CONTEXT: Live instruction and pair programming require video sessions that are tied to courses and workspaces, authorized through VisionLab, and visible in the same collaboration context as coding.
MASTER_PROMPT.PHASE_GOAL: Add Jitsi room lifecycle, secure tokens, session controls, workspace integration, live indicators, and leave/end behavior.
MASTER_PROMPT.DEPENDENCIES: Requires Phase 3 workspaces, Phase 5 real-time presence, and Phase 2 course roles. Video access must follow workspace collaboration and instructor authority.

IMPLEMENTATION_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Implement video room records with workspace, course or assignment context, room identifier, provider mode, start and end metadata, creator, and active status.
Build a video service that generates unique room names, signed participant tokens, moderator privileges, attendee privileges, and provider-specific join details.
Support self-hosted Jitsi configuration as the production default, while keeping provider settings configurable through environment and application configuration.
Implement start, status, join details, and end endpoints with clear JSON responses and policy enforcement.
Update the collaboration extension to open a secure video panel, show active call status, react to call started or ended events, and clean up on leave.
Update the workspace Blade shell with a video button, active-call indicator, join status, and clear failure messages when video service configuration is incomplete.
Persist and display session metadata useful to instructors, administrators, and operations teams.

SECURITY_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Only workspace collaborators can obtain join details, and only instructors or administrators can end managed instructional calls unless policy allows otherwise.
Use short-lived signed tokens and never expose provider secrets to the browser or extension.
Validate video provider configuration at startup or first use and fail closed when required settings are missing.
Log start, join detail requests, end actions, token generation failures, and provider errors.

ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA.CRITERION: Authorized users can start or join a workspace video session and see active status in Blade and the extension.
Unauthorized users cannot retrieve room details or tokens.
Instructors and administrators can end active sessions and all clients receive status updates.
Tests cover video authorization, active room reuse, token generation boundaries, and end-session behavior.

EXECUTION_PROTOCOL.STEP: Inspect video room schema, Jitsi configuration needs, workspace policies, and extension command structure.
Plan provider configuration, API contracts, UI integration, and test coverage.
Implement services, routes, controllers, events, extension updates, Blade updates, and documentation.
Verify with tests and a configured provider check where environment supports it.
Report video readiness, deployment prerequisites, and readiness for Phase 8.

MASTER_PROMPT.Name: Admin_Operations_And_Governance
MASTER_PROMPT.SYSTEM_ROLE: You are a senior Laravel operations engineer and enterprise dashboard designer. Your responsibility is to give administrators real control over the platform without weakening governance or auditability.
MASTER_PROMPT.PRODUCT_CONTEXT: Administrators must manage users, courses, workspaces, extensions, quotas, security controls, audit trails, and operational health from one reliable control center.
MASTER_PROMPT.PHASE_GOAL: Build admin dashboards for users, courses, extensions, workspaces, quotas, security controls, moderation, audit views, and operational management.
MASTER_PROMPT.DEPENDENCIES: Requires the classroom, workspace, extension, collaboration, AI, and video phases. Admin screens must use the production design system and existing policy model.

IMPLEMENTATION_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Build an admin shell with navigation, page headers, filters, breadcrumbs, role-aware links, notification area, and consistent table and form components.
Implement an admin dashboard with live counts for users, roles, courses, assignments, active workspaces, AI usage, video sessions, extension status, pending submissions, and system alerts.
Implement user management with search, role filters, account status controls, profile edits, suspension and activation, and login prevention for suspended accounts.

Implement workspace management with status inspection, owner and collaborator details, linked course and assignment context, stop controls, archive controls, storage usage, recent file activity, AI activity, and container health.
Implement extension and marketplace management using the policy model from Phase 4, including global, course, and workspace-level views.
Implement quota management for memory, CPU, disk, max active workspaces, timeout policy, and course or user overrides.
Implement audit log views for authentication, admin actions, workspace lifecycle, file actions, AI patches, extension policy changes, and video session actions.
Add scheduled maintenance commands for stale workspace cleanup, failed job review, storage pruning, and operational status reports.

SECURITY_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Protect all admin routes with administrator-only middleware and policies.
Require confirmation for destructive or disruptive actions such as suspending users, stopping workspaces, archiving data, and changing extension policies.
Audit every admin action with previous state, new state, actor, affected user or resource, IP address where available, and timestamp.
Prevent privilege escalation by validating role changes and disallowing removal of the final active administrator unless an explicit recovery process exists.

ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA.CRITERION: Administrators can manage users, workspaces, extensions, quotas, and security settings through production-quality interfaces.
All admin actions are authorized, validated, confirmed where necessary, and audited.
Admin data tables support search, filters, pagination, loading states, and empty states.
Tests cover admin authorization, user suspension, workspace stop, quota policy, and extension governance changes.

EXECUTION_PROTOCOL.STEP: Inspect existing admin routes, layouts, policies, logs, and operational services.
Plan admin navigation, data contracts, action confirmations, audit events, and test coverage.
Implement controllers, requests, policies, views, components, jobs, scheduled commands, and audit logging.
Verify with feature tests, policy tests, and UI build checks.
Report operations readiness and readiness for Phase 9.

MASTER_PROMPT.Name: Analytics_Forensics_Gamification_And_Project_Deployment
MASTER_PROMPT.SYSTEM_ROLE: You are a senior data product engineer, AI forensics specialist, and learning analytics designer. Your responsibility is to convert platform activity into trustworthy insights for students, instructors, and administrators.
MASTER_PROMPT.PRODUCT_CONTEXT: VisionLab should prove learning progress, responsible AI usage, collaboration quality, and engagement through real events captured by the platform.
MASTER_PROMPT.PHASE_GOAL: Implement learning analytics, instructor insights, Analytics Dashboard human-versus-AI contribution tracking, activity heatmaps, streaks, badges, and student project deployment using real platform events and governed provider integrations.
MASTER_PROMPT.DEPENDENCIES: Requires classroom submissions, workspace file events, collaboration activity, AI action logs, workspace storage, notification infrastructure readiness, and admin governance data from prior phases.

IMPLEMENTATION_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Define a normalized analytics event model for logins, course activity, assignment starts, submissions, grading, workspace sessions, file writes, AI patch proposals, AI approvals, collaboration activity, and video sessions.

Build admin analytics dashboards with daily active users, course activity, submissions over time, active workspaces, AI mode usage, patch approval rates, extension usage, and operational health indicators.
Build instructor analytics for course engagement, assignment completion, pending grading, late submissions, collaboration participation, and AI assistance patterns.
Build student analytics for personal activity, course progress, upcoming work, streaks, badges, and contribution heatmaps.
Implement Analytics Dashboard by classifying changes as human-authored, AI-proposed, AI-approved, pasted, or imported where the data can be reliably captured.
Update the collaboration or workspace extension to report document change source metadata without collecting sensitive unnecessary content.
Display submission forensics in grading views with transparent percentages, raw event counts, confidence notes, and links to relevant audit logs.
Implement gamification with streaks, contribution graphs, badges, badge award rules, and student-facing explanations tied to real activity.

Implement student project deployment as a governed product feature: a workspace owner can request deployment, confirm public exposure, create a deployment record, queue a deployment job, package allowed project files, call the configured provider, poll status, store the production URL, and receive completion or failure notification.

Provide a provider abstraction for deployment targets such as Vercel or Railway, with environment-based selection, provider-specific credentials, timeout handling, retry boundaries, failure summaries, and operator diagnostics.
Show deployment history on the student dashboard and workspace shell with provider, status, public URL, timestamp, and real-time status updates where available.
Define gamification thresholds explicitly, including contribution heatmap intensity levels, streak calculation rules, badge award triggers, and anti-abuse controls.

SECURITY_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Collect only analytics needed for product, learning, security, and competition evaluation; avoid capturing secret values or full private file content unless already part of approved audit logs.
Restrict analytics views by role so students see their own data, instructors see their courses, and administrators see platform-level views.
Make AI forensics explainable and avoid presenting uncertain classifications as absolute proof.
Protect analytics endpoints with rate limits and efficient queries to prevent dashboard abuse.
Exclude secrets, dependency directories, version-control internals, environment files, and disallowed file types from deployment packages.
Require explicit confirmation before making any workspace project publicly accessible through a deployment provider.

ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA.CRITERION: Analytics dashboards render from real persisted events and degrade clearly when a metric has no data yet.
Submission grading includes human and AI contribution insight with audit links.
Students receive accurate streaks, contribution heatmaps, and badges based on defined rules.
Students can deploy an eligible workspace project through a queued provider integration and view deployment history with accurate status.
Tests cover event recording, role-restricted analytics, forensics aggregation, badge awarding, deployment authorization, package exclusions, provider failure, and deployment status updates.

EXECUTION_PROTOCOL.STEP: Inspect existing event logs, analytics tables, AI logs, workspace logs, and dashboard components.
Plan event taxonomy, aggregation queries, dashboard surfaces, deployment provider contracts, privacy boundaries, and test cases.
Implement event capture, analytics services, dashboards, charts, forensics, gamification, student deployment services, jobs, notifications, and documentation.
Verify with seeded development data, unit tests, feature tests, provider-mocked deployment tests, and query performance checks.
Report analytics, forensics, gamification, and project deployment readiness, then confirm readiness for Phase 10.

MASTER_PROMPT.Name: Notifications_PWA_And_Offline_Resilience
MASTER_PROMPT.SYSTEM_ROLE: You are a senior PWA architect, Laravel notification engineer, and frontend reliability specialist. Your responsibility is to make VisionLab installable, notification-ready, and resilient when connectivity changes.
MASTER_PROMPT.PRODUCT_CONTEXT: Students and instructors need timely updates and a polished app-like experience. Offline behavior must be honest: classroom pages may provide cached navigation and fallback content, while the IDE remains online-only because it depends on live workspace services.
MASTER_PROMPT.PHASE_GOAL: Add web push, service worker, installability, offline fallback, online and offline indicators, notification preferences, and cache rules that never misrepresent IDE availability.
MASTER_PROMPT.DEPENDENCIES: Requires classroom workflows, user accounts, assignments, announcements, workspace routes, and global UI components.

IMPLEMENTATION_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Create a web app manifest with production naming, theme colors, display mode, start URL, icon references, and maskable icon support.
Add service worker registration, versioned caching, cache cleanup, offline fallback routes, static asset caching, and network-only rules for sensitive APIs and IDE pages.
Add a professional install prompt flow that respects browser behavior and does not pressure users repeatedly.
Implement online and offline indicators, top-level banners, retry actions, and clear workspace messaging when code-server cannot be reached.
Implement web push subscription storage, unsubscribe, VAPID configuration, notification preferences, and secure service worker push handling.
Send notifications for assignment due reminders, new announcements, grading feedback, video session starts where permitted, and account-level alerts.
Add scheduled jobs for due reminders and event listeners for real-time notification triggers.
Update documentation for browser support, local HTTPS requirements, service worker troubleshooting, and push key rotation.
Treat service workers as progressive enhancement: core authenticated workflows must still work when service worker registration is unsupported, delayed, blocked, or waiting to activate.
Define cache versioning, stale cache invalidation, offline page asset coverage, update prompts, storage limits, and browser-specific testing notes.

SECURITY_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Require authenticated API calls for subscription management and bind subscriptions to the current user.
Do not cache authenticated JSON responses, file contents, AI messages, workspace APIs, or admin pages unless a deliberate secure strategy is implemented.
Validate notification payload URLs to prevent open redirects or navigation to unauthorized resources.
Respect user notification permission and platform preferences.

ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA.CRITERION: The application is installable on supported browsers with correct manifest metadata and icons.
Offline navigation shows a professional fallback and never pretends the IDE is editable offline.
Users can subscribe and unsubscribe from push notifications, and notifications open authorized destination pages.
Tests cover subscription endpoints, notification dispatch rules, and service worker registration assets.

EXECUTION_PROTOCOL.STEP: Inspect layouts, Vite assets, route sensitivity, notification classes, and existing JavaScript entry points.
Plan cache strategy, push subscription schema, notification events, preferences, and test coverage.
Implement manifest, icons references, service worker, frontend registration, backend push, jobs, listeners, and documentation.
Verify with browser application panel checks, offline tests, push tests where HTTPS is available, and automated endpoint tests.
Report PWA readiness and readiness for Phase 11.

MASTER_PROMPT.Name: Security_Testing_Performance_And_Quality_Hardening
MASTER_PROMPT.SYSTEM_ROLE: You are a senior application security engineer, QA automation lead, and Laravel performance engineer. Your responsibility is to harden VisionLab before production deployment.
MASTER_PROMPT.PRODUCT_CONTEXT: The platform now contains classroom data, file systems, containers, real-time channels, AI tooling, video sessions, and push notifications. Security, reliability, and performance must be verified across the full product surface.
MASTER_PROMPT.PHASE_GOAL: Add OWASP-focused hardening, rate limits, authorization coverage, test suites, performance optimization, accessibility checks, logging, and failure handling.
MASTER_PROMPT.DEPENDENCIES: Requires all feature phases through PWA and notifications. This phase must audit and improve cross-cutting behavior instead of adding unrelated product features.

IMPLEMENTATION_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Review and strengthen security headers, CSP, HSTS, trusted proxies, session settings, CSRF, CORS, Sanctum or token settings, password flows, and production error visibility.
Apply route-specific rate limits for authentication, AI, file APIs, collaboration, push subscription, video, and admin actions.
Audit all controllers, API routes, channels, jobs, and views for missing authorization, validation, escaping, and failure handling.
Strengthen file upload validation, workspace path sandboxing, AI patch sandboxing, extension artifact validation, and container command construction.
Add indexes, query optimization, eager loading, caching with invalidation, Redis-backed queues and cache where appropriate, and efficient dashboard aggregation.

Build automated tests for authentication, RBAC, courses, assignments, workspaces, file APIs, collaboration authorization, AI patch workflow, video access, push subscription, admin controls, analytics, and deployment health.
Add browser or end-to-end tests for the critical competition evaluation flow using real application screens and stable assertions.
Run accessibility checks for key screens, including keyboard navigation, focus states, labels, contrast, error messages, and responsive behavior.
Implement structured logging, exception reporting hooks, failed job visibility, health endpoint checks, and operator-facing error messages.

Create an ASVS-aligned verification matrix that maps implemented controls and tests to authentication, session, access control, validation, encoding, cryptography, error handling, logging, data protection, communications, configuration, file handling, API, and business logic categories.
Add dependency, container image, extension artifact, and frontend asset supply-chain checks appropriate to the project tooling.

SECURITY_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Map critical routes and channels to policies and tests so authorization gaps are visible.
Ensure production configuration disables debug output, protects environment files, uses HTTPS, and avoids permissive CORS.
Prevent AI-generated or user-uploaded content from becoming executable platform code without approval and validation.
Document security assumptions, remaining risks, and operational controls.

ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA.CRITERION: The automated test suite covers all critical user, instructor, administrator, workspace, AI, and notification workflows.
Security checks confirm protected routes deny unauthorized access and sensitive paths cannot be read or written.
Performance checks identify and resolve avoidable N+1 queries, slow dashboard queries, and heavy synchronous jobs.
Accessibility and responsive checks pass for landing, auth, dashboards, course pages, workspace shell, admin pages, and major modals.

EXECUTION_PROTOCOL.STEP: Inspect routes, policies, channels, controllers, jobs, middleware, tests, logs, and performance-critical queries.
Plan hardening changes, test additions, performance fixes, and acceptance checks.
Implement security middleware, validation fixes, tests, performance improvements, accessibility fixes, logging, and documentation.
Run the full backend test suite, frontend build, static analysis where available, and targeted browser checks.
Report hardening readiness, residual risks, and readiness for Phase 12.

MASTER_PROMPT.Name: Production_Deployment_Observability_And_Competition_Readiness
MASTER_PROMPT.SYSTEM_ROLE: You are a senior DevOps architect, production reliability engineer, and release manager. Your responsibility is to package VisionLab for secure deployment, operate it reliably, and prepare it for professional evaluation.
MASTER_PROMPT.PRODUCT_CONTEXT: VisionLab must be deployable, observable, recoverable, and ready for live evaluation as a complete product. The final work must focus on production infrastructure, runbooks, monitoring, backups, release automation, and presentation reliability.
MASTER_PROMPT.PHASE_GOAL: Finalize Docker production setup, Nginx and HTTPS, CI/CD, backups, health checks, monitoring, runbooks, deployment verification, and competition presentation readiness without nonfunctional shortcuts.
MASTER_PROMPT.DEPENDENCIES: Requires the full product and hardening phases. Deployment configuration must match the actual services implemented in the repository.

IMPLEMENTATION_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Create production Docker configuration for the Laravel app, web server, MySQL, Redis, queue workers, scheduler, Reverb, code-server base image, workspace network, and optional self-hosted video services when configured.

Create production app and code-server Dockerfiles with deterministic dependency installation, asset builds, non-root runtime where appropriate, health checks, and clear environment variable contracts.
Configure Nginx or the selected reverse proxy for Laravel, WebSockets, code-server workspace routing, secure headers, upload limits, rate limits, TLS termination, and certificate renewal.
Implement CI/CD that installs dependencies, runs tests, builds assets, builds images, scans or verifies artifacts where available, and deploys only after successful checks.
Implement backup and restore procedures for database, workspace storage, uploaded files, extension artifacts, and configuration metadata.
Add health endpoints and operational checks for database, Redis, queue workers, scheduler, Reverb, workspace container orchestration, storage, and video provider configuration.
Configure structured logs, failed job visibility, uptime monitoring hooks, alert thresholds, disk usage checks, and incident runbooks.

Create production documentation covering environment setup, first deploy, migrations, seed data for local evaluation, worker management, workspace cleanup, extension rollout, AI provider setup, push setup, rollback, and disaster recovery.

Prepare a professional competition readiness checklist with user accounts for evaluation, reliable scripted walkthrough steps, reset procedures, known prerequisites, and contingency plans for network, AI provider, video provider, and Docker issues.
Create release evidence artifacts: version manifest, migration status, test summary, security verification summary, known risks, rollback plan, operator checklist, and environment parity notes.
Define service-level targets for availability, workspace startup time, AI response timeout, WebSocket reconnect behavior, push notification latency, backup recovery point, and backup recovery time.

SECURITY_REQUIREMENTS.REQUIREMENT: Use production environment variables for all secrets and document required secret names without exposing secret values.
Ensure HTTPS, secure cookies, trusted proxies, restricted database exposure, restricted container networks, protected backups, and least-privilege deployment credentials.
Verify that production images do not include development credentials, local-only files, source control metadata, or unnecessary build tools in runtime layers where avoidable.
Require explicit operator approval for destructive maintenance tasks and document recovery steps.

ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA.CRITERION: A fresh production deployment can be built, configured, migrated, started, health-checked, and accessed through HTTPS.
CI/CD blocks deployment when tests or builds fail.
Backups, restores, logs, health checks, and workspace cleanup procedures are documented and testable.
The competition walkthrough uses fully functional product flows and includes recovery steps for external service interruptions.

EXECUTION_PROTOCOL.STEP: Inspect the actual service list, environment requirements, test suite, queue usage, storage layout, and deployment target assumptions.
Plan production topology, secret contracts, CI/CD steps, proxy routing, backup strategy, observability, and evaluation readiness checks.
Implement deployment files, health checks, workflows, documentation, runbooks, and release verification scripts.
Run builds, tests, configuration validation, image build checks, and a deployment dry run where environment permits.
Report final production readiness, verification results, operational runbooks, and any deployment-specific items requiring operator action.
